title: Arrays & Loops
description: Work with Java arrays, ArrayList, iteration patterns, and 2D arrays for interview problems.

sections.title: int[] Arrays: Declaration and Access
sections.explanation: Java arrays are fixed-size, zero-indexed. Declare with `int[] arr = new int[size]` or `int[] arr = {1, 2, 3}`. Access with `arr[i]`, get length with `arr.length` (no parentheses). Out-of-bounds access throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. Arrays are reference types—assigning one array variable to another copies the reference, not the data.

exercise.prompt: Create an int array `scores` with values {85, 92, 78, 95, 88}. Print the first element, the last element (use scores.length - 1), and the total length.

validation.message: Print first element (85), last element (88), and length (5) on separate lines.
Declare an int[] array and use .length for the size.

exercise.hint: Use int[] scores = {85, 92, 78, 95, 88}. Access last with scores[scores.length - 1].

sections.title: ArrayList: Dynamic Arrays
sections.explanation: ArrayList<Integer> is a resizable array. Add with `.add(val)`, access with `.get(i)`, update with `.set(i, val)`, remove with `.remove(i)`, size with `.size()`. Must use wrapper types (Integer, not int). Import `java.util.ArrayList`. In interviews, use ArrayList when the size is unknown upfront.

exercise.prompt: Create an ArrayList<String> called `names`. Add "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie". Print the size, then print the element at index 1. Remove the first element and print the list.

validation.message: Print size (3), element at index 1 (Bob), then the list after removing index 0.
Use ArrayList with add(), get(), and remove() methods.

exercise.hint: list.get(1) returns the second element. list.remove(0) removes the first element.

sections.title: For Loops and Enhanced For-Each
sections.explanation: Standard for loop: `for (int i = 0; i < arr.length; i++)`. Enhanced for-each: `for (int x : arr)` iterates over elements directly—cleaner but no index access. Use standard for when you need the index (e.g., comparing adjacent elements). Use for-each when you just need values. Both work on arrays and Collections.

exercise.prompt: Given int[] arr = {3, 7, 2, 9, 4}, use a standard for loop to find the sum of all elements and print it. Then use an enhanced for-each loop to find and print the minimum value.

validation.message: Sum should be 25, minimum should be 2.
Use a standard for(int i=...) loop and an enhanced for-each loop.

exercise.hint: Initialize sum to 0 and add each element. Initialize min to arr[0] and update if element is smaller.

sections.title: 2D Arrays
sections.explanation: 2D arrays are arrays of arrays: `int[][] grid = new int[rows][cols]`. Access with `grid[row][col]`. Rows: `grid.length`, columns: `grid[0].length`. Nested loops iterate row-by-row. Common in matrix and grid interview problems (DFS/BFS on grids, dynamic programming tables).

exercise.prompt: Create a 2x3 grid: {{1,2,3},{4,5,6}}. Print the number of rows, number of columns, and the element at row 1 col 2. Then print the sum of all elements using nested for loops.

validation.message: Use int[][] for 2D array and .length for dimensions.

exercise.hint: grid.length gives rows, grid[0].length gives cols. Nested loops accumulate the sum.
